_comment: Golden cases for the LIVE consolidation eval (app/evals/live_consolidate.py). Each case is ONE candidate pair of dimension definitions, run through the REAL production consolidation confirm prompt+model, graded merge/keep against `metadata.expected` by exact match (categorical → no judge tier; see docs/ai-evals.md 'Grader architecture'). Fields grouped by consumer (docs/eval-case-schema.md): `metadata` is harness-only; `given.pair` is the two descriptors the confirm call compares; `judge` (optional) marks a case for the periodic label-audit. Definitions are exact slices of real runs (see each metadata.source). Spans easy (clear merge/keep) + hard-but-decided (contested, high-correlation confounds).
judge_background: Consolidation compares TWO dimension definitions that scored applicants near-identically, and decides whether they are the SAME underlying concept (merge) or genuinely distinct axes that should stay apart (keep). Judge the definitions themselves: would they score the same applicant the same way, for the same reason? Merge only when they measure one thing; keep apart when a plausible applicant would land genuinely high on one and low on the other for a real reason (a faint or edge-case difference is not enough).

metadata.note: Clean, unambiguous merge — both definitions measure pet count/type and its effect on communal living.
metadata.label_rationale: MERGE: both definitions score the same applicant the same way for the same reason — pet count/type and its effect on shared spaces. No concrete applicant lands high on one and low on the other. The clean non-contested MERGE.
metadata.source: Rank run 5 (2026-07-16), consolidate_audit pair pet_ownership <- pet_situation. pet_ownership def from run 3 (kept/older key); pet_situation def recovered from run 5's raw discovery report (worker 1).

pair.name: Pet ownership
pair.definition: The number and type of pets in the household, which affects shared outdoor spaces, common areas, allergen exposure among neighbours, and the practical management of communal living.

pair.name: Pet situation
pair.definition: The nature and number of pets the household brings, relevant to shared-space use, neighbour comfort, and any co-op pet policies.

metadata.note: High-correlation confound: values/ideology (A) vs. social behaviour (B) — distinct axes that co-occur.
metadata.label_rationale: KEEP: the pair correlates at r=0.84 (people drawn to co-ops score high on both) but measures different things — values/ideology (A) vs. social behaviour (B). A household can hold strong co-op values yet prefer a private home life (high A, low B), or be intensely social without ideological commitment (low A, high B). Tests judge resistance to over-merging on correlation alone, from definitions.

provenance.note: Source run predates provenance capture (2026-07-16); definitions are an exact slice of the committed rank_baseline.json fixture.

pair.name: Co-operative values alignment
pair.definition: The extent to which the applicant articulates a genuine, intrinsic alignment with co-operative values — mutual aid, democratic governance, shared responsibility, community accountability — as distinct from purely instrumental motivations such as affordability or housing security.

pair.name: Communal social orientation
pair.definition: The degree to which the household frames its home life as embedded in shared, communal living — actively seeking neighbourly relationships, informal mutual care, and shared social life — versus preferring a private, self-contained household that happens to share a building.

metadata.note: High-correlation confound: writing quality (A) vs. behavioural reliability (B).
metadata.label_rationale: KEEP: the pair correlates at r=0.887 but is a confound — writing quality (A) and behavioural reliability (B) are different axes. A reliable but inarticulate applicant scores high on B and low on A (and inverse). Merging would conflate 'writes well' with 'does the work'.
metadata.source: Rank run 4 (2026-07-16), consolidate_audit pair essay_specificity_and_concreteness ~ follow_through_reliability (KEEP; essay_specificity def from run 4, follow_through_reliability def recovered from run 1).

pair.name: Essay specificity and concreteness
pair.definition: The degree to which the household's essays cite specific past actions, named roles, concrete outcomes, and verifiable evidence rather than generic claims, vague platitudes, or corporate-filler language.

pair.name: Follow-through reliability
pair.definition: Evidence — from prior roles rather than stated intention — that the household consistently completes tasks, closes loops, and does the unglamorous low-visibility work that sustains a volunteer-run organisation.

metadata.note: CONTESTED — both verdicts defensible; leaning merge. Not an answer key; consistency across runs is the signal, not direction.
metadata.label_rationale: CONTESTED — both verdicts are defensible from the definitions alone, so this is review material, not an answer key. LEANING merge: at r=0.925 almost no applicant diverges, so treating them as one axis for this pool loses little, and the production prompt is stable on merge (confirmed by a live-stability run). The KEEP reading is also coherent on a definitions-first principle (formal certification for legally-regulated work vs. practical breadth are genuinely different things). Agreement is not pass/fail here; consistency across repeated runs is.
metadata.source: Rank run 6 (2026-07-16), consolidate_audit pair licensed_trade_skills <- hands_on_trade_skills. licensed_trade_skills def from runs 3/4 (stable); hands_on_trade_skills def from run 5.

pair.name: Licensed trade skills
pair.definition: The extent to which the household holds formal trade qualifications (electrical, plumbing, mechanical, masonry, arboriculture) that allow legally-regulated building-system repairs to be performed in-house without a contractor.

pair.name: Hands-on trade skills
pair.definition: The depth and breadth of licensed or practised physical-trade skills (electrical, plumbing, masonry, mechanics, arboriculture, upholstery, carpentry, etc.) the household can contribute to building maintenance and repair, reducing reliance on outside contractors.

metadata.note: High-correlation confound (r=0.858): philosophical motivation (A) vs. active social behaviour (B) — distinct axes that co-occur.
metadata.label_rationale: KEEP: the pair correlates at r=0.858 but is a confound. A is philosophical motivation (why the applicant wants co-op living); B is active social behaviour (do they host, organise, cultivate neighbourly bonds). An ideologically committed but introverted applicant scores high on A and low on B. Philosophical motivation and active social investment are distinct axes; merging would hide whether fit rests on values or sociability. Tests judge resistance to over-merging correlated-but-distinct axes, reasoning from definitions alone.
metadata.source: Rank run 4 (2026-07-16), consolidate_audit pair cooperative_disposition ~ community_building_orientation (KEEP); both definitions from run 4's dimension_report.

pair.name: Co-operative disposition
pair.definition: The degree to which the household articulates genuine philosophical commitment to mutual aid, democratic governance, and shared responsibility as motivating their application — as opposed to treating co-op membership primarily as an instrumental housing vehicle.

pair.name: Community-building orientation
pair.definition: The household's active investment in cultivating social bonds with neighbours — hosting, organising events, welcoming newcomers, building genuine interpersonal relationships — as evidenced by current social habits and articulated aspirations, beyond minimum co-op compliance.
